cheatSheet:
# HTML & CSS — Quick Reference

## Semantic HTML5 Elements
```html
<header>  — site/section header
<nav>     — navigation links
<main>    — primary content (one per page)
<article> — self-contained content
<section> — thematic grouping
<aside>   — tangential/sidebar content
<footer>  — site/section footer
<figure> / <figcaption> — media + caption
<time datetime="2024-01-01">  — machine-readable date
<mark>    — highlighted text
<details>/<summary> — disclosure widget
```

## CSS Box Model
```
content → padding → border → margin
box-sizing: border-box  — width includes padding + border (use this always)
box-sizing: content-box — width is content only (browser default)
```

## Flexbox
```css
.container { display: flex; flex-direction: row | column;
  justify-content: flex-start | center | space-between | space-around | space-evenly;
  align-items: stretch | center | flex-start | flex-end | baseline;
  flex-wrap: nowrap | wrap; gap: 1rem; }
.item { flex: 1; /* flex-grow: 1, flex-shrink: 1, flex-basis: 0% */ }
```

## CSS Grid
```css
.grid { display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); /* 3 equal columns */
  grid-template-columns: 200px auto 1fr;  /* mixed */
  gap: 1rem 2rem; /* row-gap column-gap */ }
.item { grid-column: 1 / 3; grid-row: 1 / 2; }
/* Named areas */
.layout { grid-template-areas: 'header header' 'sidebar main' 'footer footer'; }
```

## Responsive Design
```css
/* Mobile-first */
.container { width: 100%; padding: 1rem; }
@media (min-width: 768px) { .container { max-width: 720px; } }
@media (min-width: 1024px) { .container { max-width: 960px; } }
/* Fluid typography */
font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 1.5rem);
```

## CSS Custom Properties
```css
:root { --color-primary: #6366f1; --spacing-md: 1rem; }
.button { background: var(--color-primary); padding: var(--spacing-md); }
```

## Positioning
```css
position: static   — normal flow (default)
position: relative — offset relative to normal position
position: absolute — removed from flow, relative to nearest positioned ancestor
position: fixed    — relative to viewport
position: sticky   — relative until scroll threshold, then fixed
```

## CSS Specificity (low to high)
Type selectors (h1) → Class selectors (.btn) → ID selectors (#nav) → Inline styles → !important

## ARIA Roles
```html
role="button"       — interactive button
role="alert"        — live region for errors
role="dialog"       — modal dialog
aria-label="Close" — accessible name
aria-expanded="true" — state
aria-describedby="hint-id" — description reference
tabindex="0"        — focusable element
tabindex="-1"       — programmatically focusable only
```

resources.title: MDN Web Docs — HTML & CSS
resources.author: Mozilla
resources.justification: The authoritative reference for all HTML elements, CSS properties, browser compatibility, and accessibility. Essential daily reference.

resources.title: CSS-Tricks — A Complete Guide to Flexbox/Grid
resources.author: CSS-Tricks
resources.justification: The best visual guides to Flexbox and Grid layout. Bookmarked by virtually every frontend developer.

resources.title: Every Layout
resources.author: Heydon Pickering & Andy Bell
resources.justification: Teaches compositional layout patterns using modern CSS. Changes how you think about layout design.

resources.title: web.dev — Learn CSS
resources.author: Google Chrome Team
resources.justification: Structured, up-to-date CSS course covering all major topics with interactive examples. Free and comprehensive.

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Can write basic HTML structure and CSS styles. Understands selectors, colors, fonts, and simple layouts.
observableSkills: Apply CSS selectors with correct specificity

milestoneProject.title: Personal Portfolio Page
milestoneProject.description: A single-page portfolio with navigation, about section, skills list, and contact form. Styled with vanilla CSS — no frameworks.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Comfortable with Flexbox, Grid, and responsive design. Understands the cascade and specificity.
observableSkills: Build responsive layouts with Flexbox and Grid
Apply media queries with mobile-first methodology
Explain CSS specificity and cascade order

milestoneProject.title: Responsive Dashboard
milestoneProject.description: A dashboard layout with a sidebar, main content area, and top navigation. Fully responsive from 320px to 1440px using Grid.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Uses CSS custom properties, animations, BEM methodology, and understands browser rendering.
observableSkills: Build complex animations with transitions and keyframes
Explain the critical rendering path and paint/reflow

milestoneProject.title: Animated Component Library
milestoneProject.description: Build 5 reusable components (modal, dropdown, accordion, tooltip, tabs) using BEM, CSS custom properties, and smooth animations.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Architects scalable CSS systems. Implements accessibility to WCAG 2.1 AA standard. Understands CSS-in-JS and utility frameworks.
observableSkills: Audit and fix WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility issues
Implement keyboard navigation for complex interactive components

milestoneProject.title: Accessible Design System
milestoneProject.description: Build a small design system: color tokens, typography scale, spacing system, and 8 accessible components (button, input, select, checkbox, modal, alert, badge, card).

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Understands CSS Houdini, Paint API, container queries, and performance at the browser engine level.
observableSkills: Use CSS Houdini Paint Worklet for custom effects
Implement container queries for truly component-scoped responsiveness
Measure and optimize Cumulative Layout Shift to < 0.1

milestoneProject.title: CSS Performance Audit
milestoneProject.description: Audit a real website's CSS. Identify unused CSS (via coverage panel), reflow-causing properties, render-blocking stylesheets, and CLS contributors. Produce a prioritized fix report.

plan.overview: Master HTML5 and CSS for software engineering interviews, covering semantic markup, layout systems, responsive design, animations, accessibility, and modern CSS architecture.
plan.skippedTopics: CSS Houdini, Paint API, and deep browser engine internals are out of scope. Focus is on interview-relevant topics up to WCAG accessibility and CSS architecture.

sessions.title: HTML5 Semantics — Elements, Accessibility, SEO
sessions.paretoJustification: Semantic HTML is the foundation of accessible, SEO-friendly web development. Interviewers test this to distinguish developers who know the full platform.
sessions.content:
## Session 1: HTML5 Semantics HTML semantics means choosing elements for their meaning, not their appearance. Semantic markup improves accessibility, SEO, maintainability, and default browser behaviors. ### Why Semantics Matter - **Screen readers** announce element roles to users — a ` View Products Download PDF
What is the return policy?

You can return items within 30 days of purchase for a full refund.

``` ### Meta Tags for SEO ```html Buy Widgets Online | WidgetStore ``` ### Java Implementation ```java import java.util.*; public class Main { // HTML5 Semantics — Elements, Accessibility, SEO — Key concepts demonstration public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("=== HTML5 Semantics — Elements, Accessibility, SEO ==="); // Example: core data structures used in this topic Map config = new LinkedHashMap<>(); config.put("topic", "HTML5 Semantics — Elements, Accessibility, SEO"); config.put("complexity", "O(n)"); config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)"); for (var entry : config.entrySet()) { System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue()); } // Example: typical operations List data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5); System.out.println("Input: " + data); System.out.println("Size: " + data.size()); System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3)); } } ``` ### Python Implementation ```python from typing import Dict, List, Any def demonstrate(): """HTML5 Semantics — Elements, Accessibility, SEO — Key concepts demonstration""" print(f"=== HTML5 Semantics — Elements, Accessibility, SEO ===") # Core data structures config: Dict[str, Any] = { 'topic': 'HTML5 Semantics — Elements, Accessibility, SEO', 'complexity': 'O(n)', 'space': 'O(1)', } for k, v in config.items(): print(f'{k}: {v}') # Typical operations data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] print(f'Input: {data}') print(f'Size: {len(data)}') print(f'Contains 3: {3 in data}') if __name__ == '__main__': demonstrate() ```
objectives: Use semantic HTML5 elements correctly based on content type and meaning
Construct a proper heading hierarchy for a multi-section page
Choose between button and anchor elements based on behavior
Write effective meta tags for SEO and social sharing

activities.description: Take a non-semantic page (all div/span) and refactor it to use correct semantic elements: header, nav, main, article, section, aside, footer, figure, time. Validate with the W3C Validator and check with axe DevTools.
Audit a real webpage using the browser's accessibility tree (DevTools > Accessibility panel). List which elements appear as which roles. Find at least 3 semantics issues and propose fixes.

reviewQuestions: Why should you use a <button> element instead of a <div> with a click handler?:::A <button> provides built-in browser behaviors for free: keyboard focusability (Tab to navigate), Enter and Space activation, proper cursor (pointer), click event on keyboard activation, and the button role announced by screen readers. A <div> has none of these — to replicate them, you'd need: tabindex='0', keydown listener for Enter/Space, role='button', cursor: pointer in CSS, and testing to catch all the edge cases you'll inevitably miss. Semantic elements encode behavior and intent; divs encode nothing.

What is the purpose of the lang attribute on the <html> element?:::lang='en' tells screen readers which language to use for text-to-speech synthesis — different languages have different pronunciation rules. Without it, a screen reader may read French text with English pronunciation rules, making it unintelligible. It also helps search engines understand the document language for proper indexing in language-specific results. For multilingual pages, lang can also be applied to specific elements to override the base language.

What is a canonical URL and why does it matter for SEO?:::A canonical URL (<link rel='canonical' href='...'>) tells search engines which version of a page is the 'official' one. Without it, the same content at multiple URLs (https://site.com/product, https://site.com/product?ref=home, http://site.com/product) is treated as duplicate content, splitting ranking signals. The canonical tag consolidates those signals to the specified URL. It's especially important for e-commerce sites where filter/sort parameters create many URL variations of the same product page.

What is structured data (JSON-LD) and what does it do?:::Structured data is machine-readable markup using Schema.org vocabulary, embedded as JSON-LD in a <script> tag. It tells search engines the explicit meaning of content — this is a Product, this is a Review, this is a Recipe, this is an FAQ. In return, Google may display rich snippets in search results: star ratings, prices, breadcrumbs, FAQ dropdowns. This can significantly increase click-through rates. JSON-LD is preferred over Microdata because it's placed in the <head>, not mixed with HTML content.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write a semantically correct HTML document, explain the purpose of each landmark element, choose the right interactive element, and write effective meta tags.

sessions.title: CSS Box Model & Layout — Flexbox, Grid, Positioning
sessions.paretoJustification: Box model, Flexbox, and Grid are the three pillars of CSS layout. They appear in every frontend interview and are used in every web project.
objectives: Explain the CSS box model and why box-sizing: border-box is always set
Build one-dimensional layouts with Flexbox using container and item properties
Build two-dimensional layouts with Grid including named areas and auto-fit columns
Apply positioning (relative, absolute, fixed, sticky) correctly

activities.description: Build a holy grail layout (header, footer, left sidebar, main content, right sidebar) using CSS Grid with named template areas. Make it responsive: single column on mobile, full layout on desktop.

Build a pricing card component with Flexbox: icon top, title, price, feature list (flex: 1 to push CTA button to bottom). Create a 3-card row with auto-fit Grid. Make cards equal height with button always at the bottom.

resources.title: CSS-Tricks: A Complete Guide to Flexbox
CSS-Tricks: A Complete Guide to Grid

reviewQuestions: What is the CSS box model and what is the difference between content-box and border-box?:::Every element is a rectangular box with four layers: content (the actual text/image), padding (space inside the border), border (the visible frame), and margin (space outside the element). content-box (browser default): the width/height CSS property applies only to the content area — padding and border are ADDED to the total rendered width. border-box: width/height includes padding and border — the content area shrinks to accommodate them. Always set box-sizing: border-box globally because it makes layout math predictable: a 200px wide element with padding stays 200px wide.

What is the difference between justify-content and align-items in Flexbox?:::justify-content controls alignment along the MAIN axis (horizontal in row direction, vertical in column direction). align-items controls alignment along the CROSS axis (perpendicular to the main axis). In a row-direction flex container: justify-content: space-between distributes items horizontally; align-items: center centers items vertically. In a column-direction container, the axes swap. This is a common interview gotcha — the axes are not fixed to horizontal/vertical, they depend on flex-direction.

When would you choose Grid over Flexbox?:::Flexbox is one-dimensional — it excels at distributing items in a single row or column with flexible sizing. Grid is two-dimensional — it controls both rows and columns simultaneously, letting items be placed at specific intersections. Use Grid for: page layouts (header, sidebar, main, footer), image galleries where you need precise row AND column control, dashboard cards, any layout where items need to align in both directions. Use Flexbox for: navigation bars, horizontal button groups, vertically centering a single element, wrapping tag clouds where flow is the point.

What is the difference between position: absolute and position: fixed?:::Both remove the element from normal flow and use top/right/bottom/left for positioning. absolute positions the element relative to its nearest POSITIONED ANCESTOR (an ancestor with position: relative/absolute/fixed/sticky). If no positioned ancestor exists, it's relative to the initial containing block (document body). fixed positions the element relative to the VIEWPORT — it stays in place when the page scrolls. Use absolute for: tooltips, dropdowns, overlays positioned relative to a parent. Use fixed for: sticky navigation bars, floating action buttons, cookie banners.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build any common layout pattern using Flexbox and Grid, explain the box model clearly, and apply positioning without side effects.

sessions.title: Responsive Design — Media Queries, Mobile-First, Fluid Typography
sessions.paretoJustification: Responsive design is a baseline requirement for every frontend role. Mobile-first and fluid typography are modern best practices tested in interviews.
objectives: Apply mobile-first methodology with min-width media queries
Implement fluid typography using clamp() for smooth scaling
Use srcset and sizes for responsive images
Implement container queries for component-level responsiveness

activities.description: Build a responsive navigation: hamburger menu on mobile (no JS toggle — use the checkbox hack or focus-within), horizontal menu on tablet, mega menu on desktop. Mobile-first CSS only.

Convert a design that uses fixed pixel typography (16px body, 24px h2, 48px h1) to fluid typography using clamp(). Verify in DevTools that text scales smoothly across all viewport sizes without sudden jumps.

reviewQuestions: What is mobile-first design and why is it preferred over desktop-first?:::Mobile-first means writing base CSS for small screens and adding complexity via min-width media queries as the viewport grows. Desktop-first uses max-width media queries to scale DOWN. Mobile-first is preferred because: (1) mobile traffic is now majority; (2) it forces design prioritization — with limited space, you decide what matters most; (3) smaller CSS payload for mobile devices (base styles only, no overriding); (4) progressive enhancement — start with what works everywhere, add enhancements for capable devices.

What does clamp(min, preferred, max) do in CSS?:::clamp() returns the preferred value, clamped between a minimum and maximum. clamp(1rem, 4vw, 2rem) returns 4vw unless it's less than 1rem (floor) or greater than 2rem (ceiling). Used for fluid typography: the font size scales with the viewport width between the min and max, eliminating media query breakpoints for font size. Formula for the preferred value: use CSS calc() to interpolate linearly between the min at the min viewport and the max at the max viewport.

What is the difference between srcset and the picture element?:::srcset is used for resolution switching — providing the same image at different resolutions for the browser to choose the most appropriate based on screen pixel density and viewport size. The browser decides which to load. picture is used for art direction — serving completely different images at different screen sizes (e.g., a wide landscape crop for desktop, a portrait close-up for mobile). You control which image displays via media attributes on <source> elements. Both can be combined on one element.

What are container queries and how do they differ from media queries?:::Media queries respond to the VIEWPORT size — the same component looks the same whether it's in a narrow sidebar or a wide main area (because the viewport is the same). Container queries respond to the PARENT CONTAINER's size — the component can be truly responsive to its context. Define the container: container-type: inline-size. Then: @container (min-width: 400px) { styles }. This enables component libraries where components adapt without knowing where they'll be placed.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build a fully responsive layout using mobile-first CSS, implement fluid typography, use responsive images correctly, and explain the difference between media and container queries.

sessions.title: CSS Advanced — Animations, Transitions, Custom Properties, Pseudo-elements
sessions.paretoJustification: Animations and custom properties are used in every modern design system. Understanding when to use CSS vs JS animation is a senior-level distinction.
objectives: Define and use CSS custom properties for a design token system with dark mode support
Implement smooth transitions on the correct properties (transform, opacity) for performance
Build CSS keyframe animations with staggering, fill-mode, and prefers-reduced-motion support
Use pseudo-elements and modern pseudo-classes (:is, :where, :has, :focus-visible)

activities.description: Build a dark/light mode toggle using only CSS custom properties and a data-theme attribute on the HTML element. No JavaScript color changes — only toggling the attribute class and letting CSS variables do the work.

Animate a card grid: cards fade-in-up on page load with staggered delays. Add a loading skeleton animation. Add a button hover with lift + shadow transition. All animations must respect prefers-reduced-motion.

reviewQuestions: Which CSS properties are safe to animate for performance and why?:::Only transform and opacity are safe to animate for performance. Animating these properties is handled by the browser's compositor thread — the GPU composites layers without the main thread or layout engine being involved, resulting in smooth 60fps animations. Animating any property that causes reflow (width, height, padding, margin, top, left, font-size) forces the browser to recalculate layout for the entire affected subtree on every frame — this is expensive and causes janky animations. Use transform: translateX() instead of left; transform: scale() instead of width.

What is the difference between CSS transitions and CSS animations?:::Transitions respond to STATE CHANGES — they interpolate between two values when a CSS property changes (usually triggered by :hover, :focus, class toggle, or JS). They require a start state, a trigger, and an end state — they cannot loop or have multiple keyframes. Animations (via @keyframes) are self-contained — they run automatically, can loop infinitely, have multiple steps (keyframes), run forwards/backwards, and can be paused/reversed. Use transitions for simple hover effects and state changes; use animations for looping effects, complex sequences, and self-starting animations.

What does prefers-reduced-motion do and why must you implement it?:::@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) detects whether the user has enabled 'Reduce Motion' in their OS settings. Some users experience motion sickness, vestibular disorders, or seizures triggered by animations. Implementing this query is a WCAG 2.1 accessibility requirement. Best practice: define animations normally, then in the media query either disable them entirely (animation: none), shorten duration to near-zero, or substitute a cross-fade instead of a slide/bounce for users who are sensitive to motion.

What is a CSS custom property and how does it differ from a preprocessor variable like SCSS $variable?:::CSS custom properties (--name: value) are live values that cascade and inherit like regular CSS properties. They can be changed at runtime with JavaScript, overridden in any scope (:root, .component, :hover), and updated with @media queries. SCSS variables are compile-time constants — they are substituted during the build process and result in static CSS values. Once compiled, a SCSS variable cannot be changed without recompilation. CSS custom properties enable dynamic theming (dark mode, user color preferences) that SCSS variables cannot achieve.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement a design token system with CSS custom properties, build accessible animations with prefers-reduced-motion support, and explain which properties to animate for performance.

sessions.title: Forms & Validation — Input Types, Accessibility, JS Validation
sessions.paretoJustification: Forms are in every web application. Accessible, validated forms are a mark of quality and tested in frontend interviews.
objectives: Build a semantically correct form with proper label associations and ARIA attributes
Use appropriate input type for each data type
Implement JavaScript validation that announces errors to screen readers
Handle form submission with loading states and server error display

activities.description: Build a multi-step form: step 1 (account info), step 2 (personal details), step 3 (confirmation). Validate each step before advancing. Show a progress indicator. Announce step changes to screen readers using aria-live.
Audit an existing form with axe DevTools or NVDA. List all accessibility issues (missing labels, poor error messages, wrong input types). Fix all issues and re-audit to verify.

resources.title: MDN: Web forms — Working with user data

reviewQuestions: Why should you always use a <label> element associated with every input?:::Labels provide the accessible name for the input — announced by screen readers when the user focuses the field. Without a label, a screen reader might only announce 'edit text' with no context. Labels also increase the click target — clicking the label focuses the input, which is especially helpful for checkboxes and radio buttons. Use for-id association (<label for='email'> + id='email') or nesting the input inside the label. Never use placeholder as a substitute for a label — placeholder disappears when the user starts typing.

What is novalidate and when should you use it?:::Adding novalidate to a form element disables the browser's built-in HTML5 validation (the native popup error messages). Use it when you're implementing custom JavaScript validation — this gives you full control over error presentation, allowing you to use your design system's error styles rather than browser-default popups. You still use the HTML5 attributes (required, type='email', minlength) for programmatic constraint validation via the Constraint Validation API (field.validity.valueMissing, field.checkValidity()), but suppress the native UI.

How should validation errors be communicated to screen reader users?:::Use role='alert' or aria-live='polite' on the error message container — this causes screen readers to announce the message when it appears without the user needing to navigate to it. Link the input to its error with aria-describedby='error-id'. Set aria-invalid='true' on the input when it has an error so screen readers announce it as invalid when focused. Move focus to the first invalid field on submit. Use aria-live='polite' for form-level errors; role='alert' (assertive) only for urgent errors that require immediate attention.

What is autocomplete and why does it matter for both UX and accessibility?:::The autocomplete attribute hints to the browser (and password managers) what type of data the field expects, enabling autofill from the user's profile. Correct values: name, email, current-password, new-password, street-address, postal-code, country, tel, cc-number, etc. This significantly reduces the effort to complete forms — especially on mobile. For accessibility: autocomplete is a WCAG 2.1 AA requirement for fields collecting personal data. For security: use autocomplete='new-password' for password creation to prevent the browser from suggesting the current password.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build an accessible, validated form with proper label associations, ARIA error announcements, and correct input types.

sessions.title: CSS Architecture — BEM, SCSS, CSS Modules, Tailwind
sessions.paretoJustification: CSS architecture is critical at scale. Every team uses one of these approaches. Understanding BEM and the trade-offs between methodologies is regularly asked.
objectives: Write BEM-compliant CSS for a component with block, element, and modifier classes
Use SCSS mixins, functions, and nesting without excessive depth
Implement CSS Modules in a React component with conditional class names
Understand Tailwind's utility-first approach and when to extract to components

activities.description: Build the same card component in all four approaches: plain CSS (BEM), SCSS (with nesting and mixins), CSS Modules (React), and Tailwind. Compare file size, class name readability, and maintainability.
Take an existing monolithic CSS file with high specificity selectors. Refactor it to BEM naming convention. Identify and remove duplicate styles. Measure specificity reduction.

reviewQuestions: What problem does BEM solve and what are its naming conventions?:::BEM (Block Element Modifier) solves CSS specificity wars and naming collisions. Without a convention, developers write nested selectors (.card .title) that create high specificity — making styles hard to override. BEM enforces flat, single-class selectors: .block (standalone component), .block__element (part of the block), .block--modifier (variant of the block). All selectors have equal specificity (one class), making styles predictable and overridable. The double underscore and double hyphen make the structure immediately visible from the class name.

What is the difference between CSS Modules and Tailwind CSS?:::CSS Modules are regular CSS files where class names are locally scoped at build time — the bundler generates unique names like .Button_primary__abc123, preventing collisions. You write regular CSS syntax and import classes as objects. Tailwind is utility-first — it provides pre-built single-purpose classes (bg-blue-500, text-lg, flex) that you compose directly in HTML. CSS Modules keeps CSS separate from markup; Tailwind eliminates separate CSS files. CSS Modules is familiar CSS; Tailwind requires learning a utility vocabulary but eliminates naming decisions.

Why should SCSS nesting be limited to 2-3 levels?:::Each level of nesting increases selector specificity and creates longer selectors in the compiled CSS. .nav .menu .item .link generates a specificity of 4 classes and can only be overridden with even higher specificity. This leads to the specificity wars that BEM was designed to avoid. Deep nesting also makes CSS brittle — refactoring the HTML structure breaks the CSS. Follow the single-class rule: use nesting only for pseudo-classes/elements and modifiers on the same element, not for descendent elements.

When should you NOT use Tailwind CSS?:::Tailwind may not be ideal when: (1) the HTML is not authored by developers (CMS, user-generated content) — you can't add Tailwind classes to dynamic content; (2) your team strongly prefers separation of concerns (CSS separate from HTML); (3) you need truly dynamic styles that depend on runtime data values — Tailwind is static classes, not inline styles; (4) the project already uses a different well-established system and migration cost is high. Tailwind excels in React/Next.js projects where components co-locate HTML and styles.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement a component using BEM, SCSS, CSS Modules, and Tailwind, explain the trade-offs of each, and choose the appropriate approach for a given context.

sessions.title: Browser Rendering — CSSOM, Reflow, Repaint, Critical Path
sessions.paretoJustification: Understanding browser rendering separates senior engineers. Reflow/repaint and the critical rendering path appear in performance-focused interviews.
objectives: Describe the critical rendering path from HTML to pixels
Identify render-blocking resources and apply async/defer/preload correctly
Distinguish reflow, repaint, and composite — and list what triggers each
Fix layout thrashing by batching DOM reads and writes

activities.description: Use Chrome DevTools Performance panel to record a page load. Identify: render-blocking resources, long layout tasks, paint events. Apply one optimization (inline critical CSS, defer JS) and compare the waterfall before and after.
Identify layout thrashing in a JavaScript animation that reads/writes layout on every frame. Fix it using batched reads and writes. Compare FPS in Chrome Performance panel before and after.

resources.title: CSS Triggers (list of properties and their effects)

reviewQuestions: What is the critical rendering path and what can block it?:::The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps the browser must complete to render the first pixel: parse HTML → build DOM, parse CSS → build CSSOM, combine into render tree, calculate layout, paint pixels. CSS is render-blocking — the browser will not paint until all CSS is parsed (because CSS can affect any element). JavaScript in <script> tags without defer or async is also render-blocking and HTML-parsing-blocking. Minimizing render-blocking resources (inline critical CSS, defer non-critical JS) reduces Time to First Contentful Paint.

What is the difference between defer and async on script tags?:::Both download the script without blocking HTML parsing. async executes the script AS SOON AS it's downloaded — execution order is not guaranteed and it may interrupt HTML parsing if parsing isn't complete. defer executes scripts in the ORDER they appear in the HTML, after the HTML is fully parsed and before the DOMContentLoaded event. Use async for independent scripts with no dependencies (analytics, ads). Use defer for scripts that depend on the DOM or on each other (most application scripts).

What is layout thrashing (forced synchronous layout)?:::Layout thrashing occurs when JavaScript alternates between reading layout properties (offsetWidth, clientHeight, getBoundingClientRect) and writing layout properties (style.width, style.height) in a tight loop. Each write invalidates the layout, and each subsequent read forces the browser to immediately recalculate layout synchronously (it can't batch the reflow). This can cause dozens of reflows per frame instead of one, dropping the frame rate drastically. Fix: batch all reads first (store in variables), then batch all writes.

Why should you prefer transform/opacity for animations over top/left or width/height?:::transform and opacity are handled by the GPU compositor thread — they can be animated at 60fps without involving the main thread or triggering layout recalculations. top/left changes trigger full layout recalculation for all affected elements (reflow), then repaint — both are expensive and block the main thread. width/height also trigger reflow. Animating transform: translateX() instead of left: and transform: scale() instead of width: gives smooth animations without layout cost.
sessions.successCriteria: Can explain the critical rendering path, identify render-blocking resources, fix layout thrashing, and explain which CSS properties are safe to animate.

sessions.title: Web Accessibility — ARIA, Screen Readers, Keyboard Navigation, Color Contrast
sessions.paretoJustification: Accessibility is a legal requirement (ADA, WCAG) and increasingly tested in interviews. Implementing accessible components demonstrates senior-level knowledge.
objectives: Apply ARIA roles, properties, and states correctly for common widget patterns
Implement keyboard focus management for modals and complex components
Verify color contrast meets WCAG 2.1 AA requirements
Use live regions to announce dynamic content changes to screen readers

activities.description: Build an accessible dropdown menu: button opens menu (aria-expanded, aria-haspopup), arrow keys navigate items, Enter selects, Escape closes and returns focus, click outside closes. Test with VoiceOver or NVDA.
Audit a web page using the axe browser extension. Fix all Critical and Serious accessibility violations. Run it again to confirm 0 violations. Document each issue and its fix.

reviewQuestions: What is the first rule of ARIA?:::Don't use ARIA if a native HTML element or attribute can do the job. Native elements have built-in accessibility semantics, keyboard interactions, and browser support without any JavaScript. role='button' on a div requires you to manually add: tabindex='0', keyboard handlers for Enter and Space, cursor: pointer, and test thoroughly. A <button> element provides all of this for free and is more robust. ARIA is for EXTENDING native HTML for complex widgets (combobox, tree, grid, tabs) that have no native equivalent.

What is a live region and when should you use role='alert' vs aria-live='polite'?:::A live region is an element whose content changes dynamically and should be announced to screen reader users without them navigating to it. aria-live='polite' queues the announcement until the user is idle — use for non-urgent updates like search result counts, form success messages, or status changes. role='alert' (equivalent to aria-live='assertive') interrupts the user immediately — use ONLY for urgent errors that require immediate attention, like form validation errors that block submission. Overusing assertive makes screen readers constantly interrupt, which is very frustrating.

What are the WCAG 2.1 AA color contrast requirements and why do they matter?:::Normal text requires a contrast ratio of 4.5:1 between text and background. Large text (18pt/24px or larger, or 14pt/18.67px bold) requires 3:1. UI components (button borders, input borders, icons) require 3:1. These ratios ensure people with low vision or color vision deficiency can read and interact with content. Tools: Chrome DevTools has a built-in contrast checker; WebAIM's contrast checker; axe DevTools flags violations automatically. WCAG AA compliance is a legal requirement in the US (ADA), EU (EN 301 549), and many other jurisdictions.

What is focus trapping in a modal dialog and how do you implement it?:::When a modal opens, keyboard focus must be constrained to the modal's interactive elements — tabbing past the last focusable element should wrap to the first, and Shift+Tab past the first should wrap to the last. Without this, users can Tab into background content obscured by the modal overlay, which is confusing and unusable. Implementation: query all focusable elements (button:not([disabled]), input, a[href], [tabindex]:not([tabindex='-1'])), listen for Tab keydown, check if focus is at the boundary, preventDefault and move focus to the other end. Return focus to the trigger element on close.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement accessible interactive components with correct ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation, focus management, and passing color contrast. Can audit and fix accessibility issues.

sessions.title: CSS-in-JS & Modern Tools — Styled Components, Emotion, PostCSS
sessions.paretoJustification: CSS-in-JS tools are used widely in React codebases. Understanding the trade-offs between approaches is a senior-level architectural question.
objectives: Write styled-components with dynamic prop-based styles and themes
Understand the runtime cost of CSS-in-JS and when it matters
Configure PostCSS with autoprefixer and cssnano
Compare CSS-in-JS, CSS Modules, and utility CSS and choose based on project needs

activities.description: Build a themed button component in both styled-components and CSS Modules. Implement three variants (primary, secondary, danger) and two sizes. Compare bundle size, TypeScript experience, and runtime performance using Chrome DevTools.
Configure PostCSS in a project: autoprefixer, cssnano for production, postcss-preset-env for CSS nesting. Write CSS using native nesting and verify it compiles to valid CSS for older browsers.

reviewQuestions: What is the main performance trade-off of runtime CSS-in-JS libraries like styled-components?:::Runtime CSS-in-JS generates styles dynamically — when a component renders with certain props, it creates a CSS rule and injects it into a <style> tag or CSSStyleSheet. This happens in JavaScript on every render cycle that produces new style combinations, adding CPU overhead on the main thread. For SSR (Next.js), extracting critical CSS from styled-components requires a server-side rendering pass. Modern apps with many components and frequent re-renders may see measurable performance impact. Zero-runtime alternatives (vanilla-extract, Linaria) extract CSS at build time, eliminating the runtime cost entirely.

Why does autoprefixer exist and why do you need it in PostCSS?:::CSS properties often require vendor prefixes to work across browsers during the time a standard is being implemented: -webkit-user-select, -moz-appearance, -ms-grid-column, etc. Writing these manually is tedious and error-prone — you'd need to track which properties need prefixes for which browser versions. Autoprefixer reads your CSS and automatically adds the appropriate vendor prefixes based on your target browser list (defined in .browserslistrc or package.json). As browsers implement standards, autoprefixer stops adding unnecessary prefixes.

What is the difference between Emotion's css prop and styled API?:::The css prop (via @jsxImportSource @emotion/react) allows you to apply Emotion styles directly on any JSX element: <div css={myStyle}>. The css() helper accepts template literals, objects, or an array of both. The styled API (import styled from '@emotion/styled') creates a new React component with styles attached — identical to styled-components API. The css prop is more co-located and flexible; styled is better when the component is reused and needs a semantic component name. Both compile to class names — there's no runtime difference.

When would you choose CSS Modules over styled-components for a React project?:::CSS Modules are preferred when: (1) performance is critical — CSS Modules have zero runtime overhead, styles are static at build time; (2) the team is more comfortable with regular CSS syntax than CSS-in-JS template literals; (3) SSR is a priority — CSS Modules extract to static files with no server-side rendering complexity; (4) migrating an existing CSS codebase — CSS Modules requires minimal changes to existing CSS. Styled-components is preferred when: dynamic prop-based styles are extensive, team wants full TypeScript integration for style props, or design tokens via ThemeProvider are needed.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement styled-components with dynamic theming, explain the runtime performance implications, configure PostCSS, and choose the right CSS approach for a given project.

sessions.title: Interview Patterns & Common Questions
sessions.paretoJustification: Direct preparation for the most frequently asked HTML/CSS interview questions closes the loop between knowledge and performance.
objectives: Calculate CSS specificity for any selector and predict cascade winners
Implement classic CSS layout patterns from memory: centering, sticky footer, truncation
Explain CSS cascade layers and their effect on specificity
Walk through the mental model for ::before and ::after

activities.description: Complete a CSS interview quiz: given 10 pairs of conflicting CSS rules, predict which wins and explain why. Cover: specificity, cascade order, inline styles, !important, layer order. Verify with the browser.
Implement 5 common CSS patterns from memory without looking anything up: centering, sticky footer, text truncation, aspect ratio video, CSS-only tooltip with triangle arrow. Time yourself.

reviewQuestions: Explain CSS specificity. Which selector wins: '#nav .link' or '.nav.active a'?:::#nav .link has specificity (1, 1, 0) = 1 ID + 1 class. .nav.active a has specificity (0, 2, 1) = 0 IDs + 2 classes + 1 type. ID specificity (1, 0, 0) always outweighs any number of class selectors. So #nav .link wins because it contains one ID. Specificity is calculated as a tuple: (IDs, classes/attributes/pseudoclasses, types/pseudoelements). Compare from left to right — the first non-zero difference determines the winner.

What are CSS cascade layers (@layer) and what problem do they solve?:::Cascade layers give developers explicit control over the cascade order independently of selector specificity. Styles in a later-declared layer always win over earlier-declared layers, regardless of specificity. This solves the problem in large codebases where third-party CSS (resets, component libraries) has high-specificity selectors that are hard to override. By placing third-party CSS in a low-priority @layer base, your application styles always take precedence without resorting to !important. Unlayered styles still win over all layers.

What are three ways to vertically center an element in CSS?:::1. Flexbox: parent gets display: flex; align-items: center (and justify-content: center for both axes). 2. Grid: parent gets display: grid; place-items: center (shorthand for align-items + justify-items). 3. Absolute + transform: child gets position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%) — requires the parent to be position: relative. 4. Modern: margin: auto on a grid or flex item, or margin-block: auto in a flex column container. The flexbox/grid approaches are preferred as they don't remove the element from flow.

What is the stacking context and what creates one?:::The stacking context determines the 3D rendering order of elements (z-index). Elements in the same stacking context are z-indexed relative to each other. A new stacking context is created by: position + z-index other than auto, opacity < 1, transform, filter, will-change, isolation: isolate, and several others. A common bug: a high z-index element inside a stacking context with a low z-index parent cannot appear above elements from an ancestor stacking context, regardless of how high its own z-index is. Use isolation: isolate to create intentional stacking contexts.
sessions.successCriteria: Can correctly predict CSS cascade winners, implement common layout patterns from memory, explain cascade layers, and handle classic interview CSS questions with confidence.

quizBank.question: Which HTML element should you use for the primary navigation of a website?
quizBank.explanation: The <nav> element is specifically designed for major navigation blocks — the landmark element that assistive technologies and search engines recognize as navigation. Not all link groups need <nav> — use it for primary navigation, secondary navigation, and breadcrumbs. Footer copyright links and social media links in an article typically don't need <nav>.

quizBank.question: What is the total width of an element with: width: 200px, padding: 20px, border: 5px, box-sizing: content-box?
quizBank.explanation: With box-sizing: content-box (the default), total width = content-width + left-padding + right-padding + left-border + right-border = 200 + 20 + 20 + 5 + 5 = 250px. This is why setting box-sizing: border-box globally is recommended — it makes the declared width equal to the total rendered width.

quizBank.question: Which property controls how flex items are distributed along the MAIN axis?
quizBank.explanation: justify-content distributes space along the main axis (horizontal in row, vertical in column). align-items aligns items on the cross axis. align-content distributes multiple lines of wrapped items on the cross axis. The 'justify' properties control the main axis; 'align' properties control the cross axis.

quizBank.question: What does the alt attribute on an image do when left empty (alt='')?
options: Shows the image filename to screen readers
Tells screen readers to skip the image (decorative)
quizBank.correctAnswer: Tells screen readers to skip the image (decorative)
quizBank.explanation: An empty alt attribute (alt='') explicitly marks an image as decorative — screen readers skip it entirely. This is correct for purely decorative images that add no informational value. The distinction: omitting alt entirely is an error (screen readers may read the src URL); alt='' is intentional and correct for decorative images; alt='description' is required for meaningful images.

quizBank.question: Which CSS selector has the HIGHEST specificity?
quizBank.explanation: Specificity is (IDs, Classes, Types). #main-title = (1, 0, 0). .container .card .title = (0, 3, 0). div h1 span = (0, 0, 3). h1.title = (0, 1, 1). ID specificity (1, 0, 0) beats any number of class selectors (0, n, 0) — one ID always wins over any combination of classes and type selectors.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of aria-live='polite' on an element?
options: Delays the element's rendering until the page is loaded
Announces content changes to screen readers after the user is idle
Prevents the element from being focused
Makes the element visible only to screen readers
quizBank.correctAnswer: Announces content changes to screen readers after the user is idle
quizBank.explanation: aria-live='polite' creates a live region — when the element's content changes, the new content is queued and announced to screen reader users after they finish their current interaction. Use 'polite' for non-urgent updates (search results count, status messages). Use role='alert' (assertive) only for urgent messages that should interrupt immediately.

quizBank.question: Which approach correctly implements a mobile-first responsive design?
quizBank.explanation: Mobile-first: write base styles for the smallest screen (no media query), then use min-width media queries to add complexity for larger screens. The correct approach hides the nav by default (mobile), then shows it with display: flex at 768px and above. The other options use max-width (desktop-first) or conditional ranges that skip mobile entirely.

quizBank.question: What is the minimum color contrast ratio required for normal text under WCAG 2.1 Level AA?
quizBank.explanation: WCAG 2.1 Level AA requires: 4.5:1 for normal text (< 18pt regular or < 14pt bold), 3:1 for large text (≥ 18pt or ≥ 14pt bold), and 3:1 for UI components and graphical objects. Level AAA requires 7:1 for normal text and 4.5:1 for large text. This ensures readability for users with low vision or color vision deficiency.

quizBank.question: Which CSS property is safe to animate for smooth 60fps performance?
quizBank.explanation: transform (and opacity) are handled by the GPU compositor thread, not the main thread — animations run at 60fps without triggering layout recalculation. width, height, and padding all trigger reflow (layout recalculation) on every frame when animated, which is expensive and causes janky animations. Use transform: translateX() instead of left, transform: scale() instead of width.

quizBank.question: What does the :focus-visible pseudo-class do differently from :focus?
quizBank.correctAnswer: :focus-visible shows focus ring only for keyboard navigation, not mouse clicks, based on browser heuristics
quizBank.explanation: :focus applies whenever the element receives focus — keyboard Tab, mouse click, or programmatic focus. :focus-visible applies only when the browser determines a visual indicator is needed — specifically for keyboard navigation. Mouse clicks on buttons typically do not trigger :focus-visible (the user can see where they clicked). This lets you show clear focus rings for keyboard users while avoiding unsightly rings for mouse users. Modern practice: remove :focus outlines and use :focus-visible for your custom focus styles.

quizBank.question: What is the primary advantage of HTML & CSS?
options: D) All of the above, depending on context
quizBank.explanation: HTML & CSS offers different advantages depending on the use case. In interviews, always discuss trade-offs rather than absolving advantages.

quizBank.question: When should you NOT use HTML & CSS?
options: B) When the team is small
C) When the overhead exceeds the benefit
quizBank.explanation: Every technology has trade-offs. HTML & CSS adds complexity that may not be justified for simple use cases. Always evaluate if the overhead is worth the benefit.

quizBank.question: What is the time complexity of the core operation in HTML & CSS?
options: D) It depends on the implementation
quizBank.explanation: The time complexity depends on the specific implementation and operation. In interviews, always clarify which operation before stating complexity.

quizBank.question: How does HTML & CSS handle failures?
options: D) Strategy depends on the system requirements
quizBank.explanation: Failure handling in HTML & CSS depends on requirements: retry for transient failures, circuit breaker for cascading failures, graceful degradation for user-facing systems.

quizBank.question: Which company popularized the use of HTML & CSS?
quizBank.explanation: HTML & CSS evolved through contributions from multiple companies. In interviews, mentioning real-world examples from different companies shows breadth of knowledge.
